abate: to lessen in intensity; die down
aberration: a departure from what is normal or expected
accept: to receive or agree to something willingly
anachronism: something out of its proper time period
ancient: very old; from the distant past
arcane: understood by few; mysterious or secret
arrogant: having an exaggerated sense of one's own importance
assiduous: showing great care and persistence; diligent
astute: shrewd; quick to understand and act
augment: to make larger by adding to it
austere: severe or strict in appearance; without comfort
barren: unable to produce growth or life; empty
belligerent: hostile and aggressive; eager to fight
brave: ready to face danger or pain; courageous
brevity: concise and exact use of words; shortness
cajole: to persuade by flattery or coaxing
capricious: given to sudden changes of mood; unpredictable
catalyst: something that triggers change without being consumed
caustic: sarcastic in a bitter way; corrosive
cautious: careful to avoid danger or mistakes
clear: easy to see, understand, or perceive
coalesce: to come together into one whole
conciliatory: intended to placate or make peace
concise: giving much information in few words; brief but clear
congenial: pleasant because suited to one's taste; agreeable
conjecture: an opinion formed without complete evidence; a guess
corroborate: to confirm or support with evidence
credulous: too ready to believe things; gullible
criticize: to point out faults; to find fault with
cryptic: having a hidden or obscure meaning
cursory: hasty and without attention to detail
dearth: a scarcity or lack of something
debacle: a sudden and complete failure; fiasco
defeat: to win victory over; to beat
deference: humble respect; yielding to another's judgment
demagogue: a leader who exploits popular prejudices
desiccate: to remove moisture; dry out completely
despot: a ruler with absolute, often cruel, power
diatribe: a forceful and bitter verbal attack
diffident: modest or shy from lack of confidence
disparage: to belittle; speak of as unimportant
eager: keenly wanting to do something; enthusiastic
early: before the usual or expected time
easy: achieved without great effort; not difficult
efficacy: the ability to produce the intended result
emulate: to imitate in order to equal or surpass
enormous: very large in size or quantity
ephemeral: lasting a very short time; fleeting
equivocate: to use ambiguous language to conceal the truth
esoteric: understood by only a small, specialized group
expedient: convenient and practical though possibly improper
fast: moving or able to move quickly
fastidious: very attentive to detail; hard to please
fertile: able to produce abundant growth or life
flexible: able to bend or adapt easily
frank: open, honest, and direct in speech
friendly: kind and pleasant; warm in manner
frugal: sparing or economical with money or resources
genuine: truly what it is said to be; authentic
happy: feeling or showing pleasure or contentment
hegemony: dominance of one group over others
iconoclast: one who attacks cherished beliefs or institutions
idiosyncrasy: a peculiar habit particular to an individual
increase: to become greater in size or number
ineffable: too great to be expressed in words
ineluctable: unable to be avoided or escaped; inevitable
inimical: tending to obstruct or harm; hostile
insidious: proceeding harmfully in a gradual, subtle way
inundate: to flood; overwhelm with things to deal with
light: bright; full of light, or of little weight
magnanimous: generous and forgiving, especially toward a rival
mercurial: subject to sudden unpredictable changes of mood
mitigate: to make less severe or painful
murky: dark and gloomy; hard to see through
mysterious: difficult to understand or explain; puzzling
nascent: just coming into existence; beginning to develop
obdurate: stubbornly refusing to change one's mind
obscure: not clearly understood or expressed; hidden
old: having lived or existed for a long time
onerous: involving a heavy burden of effort
opaque: not able to be seen through; not transparent
paucity: the presence of something in small amounts; scarcity
perfunctory: done as a routine duty; without care
pernicious: having a harmful effect in a subtle way
petty: of little importance; trivial or small-minded
placate: to make someone less angry; appease
platitude: a statement used too often to be interesting
praise: to express warm approval or admiration
precocious: having developed abilities earlier than usual
prevaricate: to speak evasively; avoid the truth
prolific: producing much fruit, work, or offspring
prudent: acting with care and good judgment
quick: moving fast or done in a short time
quiescent: in a state of inactivity; dormant
reticent: not revealing one's thoughts readily; reserved
rigid: stiff and unable to bend; inflexible
sanguine: optimistic, especially in a bad situation
scarce: in short supply; hard to find
scatter: to throw or spread in different directions
scrupulous: very careful to do what is right
slow: moving or operating at low speed
stable: firmly fixed; not likely to change
stingy: unwilling to spend or give; mean
swift: moving or able to move very fast
sycophant: a person who flatters to gain advantage
terse: brief and to the point, sometimes curtly so
transient: lasting only a short time; fleeting
transparent: able to be seen through; clear
unyielding: not giving way; firm and inflexible
victory: success in a contest or struggle
volatile: liable to change rapidly and unpredictably
voracious: having a huge appetite; devouring eagerly
zealous: showing great energy and enthusiasm for a cause
